retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mutagenesis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Mutagenesis has multiple implications in clinical medicine. This topic discusses a few, including carcinogenesis, heritable diseases, microorganism resistance, large-scale mutagenic projects, and precision medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Mitosis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) is a tumor suppressor involved in the pathogenesis of colorectal cancer. Germline mutations in APC lead to a classical form of familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP). FAP is characterized by hundreds of colonic polyps or adenomas, which have a high tendency to undergo malignant transformation. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Pathophysiology -- Mutational Mechanisms Associated With Colorectal Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: Genomic instability: This is a hallmark of CRCs and encompasses the below-mentioned mechanisms, driving tumorigenesis through the accumulation of DNA repair defects and mutations.

retrieved_docs.title: Rectal Cancer -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are three major molecular pathways linked to CRC. These are chromosomal instability, mismatch repair, and hypermethylation. The chromosomal instability pathway is an accumulation of mutations unbalancing oncogene and tumor suppressor equilibrium. This is seen in diseases such as FAP with mutations in the tumor suppressor gene adenomatous polyposis coli (APC). Cells with deficiency of DNA mismatch repair (dMMR) genes, commonly MLH1 or MSH2, accumulate errors within the genome at a more rapid rate due to the inability to correct the base mismatch. This leads to high levels of microsatellite instability (MSI-H), a hallmark of Lynch syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterococcus Infections -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Extracellular superoxide produced by E. faecalis causes DNA instability and inflammatory changes in the colonic mucosa, leading to colorectal cancer. [1] This finding was supported by a higher burden of E. faecalis in the stools of colon cancer patients than in controls.

retrieved_docs.title: Hereditary Nonpolyposis Colon Cancer (Lynch Syndrome) -- Evaluation -- Clinical Testing Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial cancer diagnosed before age 50

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, DNA Replication -- Clinical Significance -- Antitumor Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Antitumor medications also may interfere with DNA replication. Cytarabine used to treat leukemia is a pyrimidine analog that inhibits DNA polymerase. 5-Fluorouracil, used in treating colon cancer, decreases the production of thymidine which is a nucleotide needed for DNA production. Etoposide is an antitumor medication that inhibits topoisomerase II; it is used to treat solid tumors, leukemias, and lymphomas. Irinotecan inhibits topoisomerase I.

retrieved_docs.title: A Review of Hereditary Colorectal Cancers -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective screening and chemoprevention strategies are essential for reducing CRC incidence and mortality in high-risk populations, with specific approaches tailored to genetic risk factors and associated extracolonic cancers.

retrieved_docs.title: Colon Cancer Screening -- Clinical Significance -- Stool DNA Test
retrieved_docs.content: Frequency of testing: The current recommendation is once every 3 years. If positive on any of the occasions, endoscopic studies such as colonoscopy and sigmoidoscopy are recommended. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Tumor Biomarkers -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH): FISH identifies specific genetic mutations in tumor cells. [24] This technique uses fluorescent-labeled DNA probes, which hybridize to specific target sequences in cells, allowing their visualization under a fluorescence microscope. APC (adenomatous polyposis coli) and ras mutations and Her2 overexpression are identified using FISH. [25]

retrieved_docs.content: Ultraviolet and ionizing radiation

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial DNA Mutations -- Function -- Mutation Induction
retrieved_docs.content: Mutagens may be of physical, chemical, or biological origin. Mostly they act on the DNA directly, causing damage, which may result in errors during replication. Although severely damaged DNA can prevent replication and cause cell death. SOS is an example of cellular response to DNA damage that results in cell cycle arrest and induction of mutagenesis. Rec A induces SOS response by recognizing single-stranded DNA and activating mutagenic DNA polymerases (II, IV, and V). [11] The following are several classes of mutagens and their subsequent effects  :

retrieved_docs.title: Lynch Syndrome (Hereditary Nonpolyposis Colorectal Cancer) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with abnormal immunohistochemical staining or microsatellite instability whose germline testing does not reveal a mutation may have double somatic mismatch repair gene mutations in the tumor DNA or may have Lynch syndrome with incomplete penetrance. Management should be based on the patient's personal and family history in such cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 322
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents characterized by a tetracyclic quinone structure and potent cytotoxic activity. Both aklarubicin and idarubicin are identified as members of this anthracycline class, sharing similar mechanisms of action that involve intercalation into DNA, inhibition of topoisomerase II, and generation of free radicals, which together contribute to their effectiveness in the treatment of various malignancies. Recognizing aklarubicin and idarubicin as part of the broader anthracycline group helps clinicians understand their pharmacologic properties, potential toxicities such as cardiotoxicity, and appropriate therapeutic use within oncology care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1268
retrieved_docs.content: Teniposide is classified as a podophyllotoxin derivative and belongs to the group of antineoplastic drugs; as a member of this class, it is used in cancer therapy to inhibit tumor cell proliferation, typically by interfering with topoisomerase II activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Decreased mitosis is described as a physiologic effect of oxytetracyklin, indicating that exposure to this antibiotic can lead to inhibition of cellular division.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1590
retrieved_docs.content: Decreased Mitosis is identified as a physiologic effect linked to oxytetracyklin, indicating that exposure to oxytetracyklin can suppress cellular division. Meclocycline is mapped to oxytetracyklin, reflecting that meclocycline is closely related to oxytetracyklin, likely sharing similar tetracycline‑class properties and mechanisms of action. Together, these relationships suggest that both oxytetracyklin and its related compound meclocycline may exert antiproliferative effects through the inhibition of mitosis, a consideration relevant to their therapeutic use and potential side‑effect profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of chemotherapeutic agents that includes aklarubicin, daunomycin, epirubicin, idarubicin and mitoxantron. These drugs share a common mechanism of action, typically involving DNA intercalation and inhibition of topoisomerase II, which underlies their effectiveness against a variety of malignant tumors. The ET protocol is directly linked to epirubicin, being mapped from this agent, which indicates that epirubicin serves as a foundational component of the ET treatment regimen. Understanding that aklarubicin, daunomycin, epirubicin, idarubicin and mitoxantron are members of the anthracycline family helps clinicians select appropriate regimens and anticipate class‑related toxicities such as cardiotoxicity, while the association of the ET protocol with epirubicin guides its application in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1264
retrieved_docs.content: Topoisomerase 1 (TOP1) inhibitors are classified as members of the topotekan group, indicating that they share the characteristic pharmacologic properties and mechanisms associated with topotekan agents. This classification helps clarify the therapeutic context and mechanistic expectations for TOP1 inhibitors within medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: The FOLFIRI antineoplastic chemotherapy regimen directly incorporates the drug irinotecan, and irinotecan is classified as a member of the Topoisomerase 1 (TOP1) inhibitor drug class. The TOP1 inhibitor class also includes the agent topotecan. By inhibiting the enzyme topoisomerase I, both irinotecan and topotecan interfere with DNA replication in malignant cells, which underlies their antineoplastic activity. Recognizing that irinotecan is a component of FOLFIRI and that it shares mechanistic and toxicity profiles with other TOP1 inhibitors such as topotecan is important for selecting appropriate chemotherapy combinations and for anticipating class‑related adverse effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1991
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanism of action for merkaptamin is currently described as an unknown cellular or molecular interaction, indicating that the precise cellular or molecular processes by which merkaptamin exerts its effects have not yet been elucidated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 480
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents that includes aklarubicin, daunomycin, epirubicin, and idarubicin. These drugs share a common anthracycline core and are employed in the treatment of various malignancies due to their ability to intercalate DNA and inhibit topoisomerase II. Within this class, epirubicin serves as the basis for the ET protocol, indicating that the ET chemotherapy regimen is derived from or incorporates epirubicin as a key component. Understanding that aklarubicin, daunomycin, epirubicin, and idarubicin are members of the anthracycline family helps clinicians recognize their shared mechanisms, potential toxicities such as cardiotoxicity, and the rationale for selecting specific agents or protocols like the ET protocol in personalized cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 304
retrieved_docs.content: The FOLFIRI antineoplastic chemotherapy regimen incorporates irinotecan as a direct component of its formulation, indicating that irinotecan is an essential drug within the FOLFIRI combination used in cancer treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Docetaxel is classified as a member of the taxane family, which comprises antineoplastic agents used in chemotherapy for various malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1563
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents that includes the individual drugs aklarubicin, daunomycin, and idarubicin. These compounds share a common anthracycline core structure and exert their anticancer effects primarily through DNA intercalation and inhibition of topoisomerase II, leading to disruption of DNA replication and transcription. Because of their similar pharmacologic properties, aklarubicin, daunomycin, and idarubicin are grouped together under the broader category of anthracyclines and related substances, which are widely used in the treatment of various malignancies such as leukemias, lymphomas, and solid tumors. Understanding that these three agents belong to the same drug class helps clinicians anticipate shared therapeutic benefits as well as common toxicities, including cardiotoxicity and myelosuppression, thereby informing patient monitoring and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1304
retrieved_docs.content: Anthracyclines and related substances constitute a class of antineoplastic agents that share a common anthracycline core and are used primarily in chemotherapy. Within this class, the drugs aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron are recognized as individual members. All three agents intercalate into DNA, inhibit topoisomerase II, and generate free radicals, mechanisms that contribute to their cytotoxic effects against rapidly dividing cancer cells. Because they belong to the same pharmacologic group, aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron exhibit similar therapeutic indications, such as treatment of leukemias and solid tumors, as well as comparable adverse effect profiles, most notably dose‑dependent cardiotoxicity and myelosuppression. Understanding that aklarubicin, idarubicin, and mitoxantron are members of the Anthracyclines and related substances class helps clinicians anticipate their pharmacodynamics, manage shared toxicities, and make informed decisions when selecting or substituting agents within this chemotherapy category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents are classified under the category of pharmacological effects—molecular mechanisms, indicating that their therapeutic action is understood within this mechanistic framework.
